headline: Breakthrough Camera Simplifies the Hunt for Invisible Particles
meta_description: Scientists replace millions of detector parts with one block of material using light-field camera technology and AI to track elusive particles.
summary_short: Researchers at ETH Zurich have built a revolutionary particle detector that could transform how physicists search for neutrinos and dark matter.
body:
For decades, particle physicists have faced an engineering nightmare: detecting invisible particles requires vast networks of millions of tiny components. The T2K neutrino experiment in Japan, for example, relies on approximately two million detector cubes and 60,000 optical fibers to track particle movements through just two tons of material.

Now, scientists at ETH Zurich and EPFL have proposed something radically simpler. Their new detector, called PLATON, replaces all those individual components with a single unsegmented block of light-producing material paired with advanced camera technology.

The innovation draws inspiration from plenoptic cameras—devices that capture not just light intensity but also the direction light arrives from. When combined with extremely sensitive photon sensors and artificial intelligence, this approach reconstructs the three-dimensional paths of particles passing through the detector.

In laboratory tests, the prototype successfully detected electrons and tracked their positions using remarkably faint light signals—as few as five detected photons. The timing-sensitive sensor also filters out background noise, focusing only on authentic particle signals.

Simulations suggest PLATON could match today's best detectors while being far easier to manufacture and scale. The breakthrough even promises sharper medical PET scans, showing how physics innovations often ripple into unexpected applications.
